briefing.incidentSummary:
A zero-day vulnerability in a power grid SCADA system was exploited, causing a controlled blackout in an industrial zone for 6 hours.

The attacker didn't cause maximum damage — they demonstrated capability. A warning shot.

The exploit code was custom-built and vendor-specific. No patch existed. Only three people in the country had ever audited this SCADA system's codebase.
briefing.missionText:
Your Mission:

› Identify who wrote the zero-day exploit

› Determine if this was state-sponsored or rogue

› Trace the controlled nature of the attack — why stop early?

› Collect at least 4 correct evidences

› Flag the right suspect

suspects.name: Ronak S
suspects.profileNotes: One of only 3 researchers with SCADA codebase access. Discreetly sold the exploit to a foreign intelligence intermediary for $280,000 — but panicked and triggered the shutdown early, preventing full infrastructure damage. Financial trail leads to a Mauritius account.
suspects.description: Ronak S is a person of interest in this case. Their motives and involvement remain under investigation.

suspects.name: Unknown Foreign Actor
suspects.profileNotes: Purchased the exploit — was the buyer. However the technical authorship of the zero-day and the decision to trigger it belong to Ronak S. The buyer only provided direction — Ronak executed.
suspects.description: Unknown Foreign Actor is a person of interest in this case. Their motives and involvement remain under investigation.

suspects.name: Supriya K
suspects.profileNotes: Had remote access for scheduled maintenance but was on leave during the attack window. Access token was active but not used — confirms she did not execute the exploit.
suspects.description: Supriya K is a person of interest in this case. Their motives and involvement remain under investigation.

suspects.name: Devraj M
suspects.profileNotes: Had warned about this vulnerability 2 years ago in an internal memo that was ignored. No current system access. His old memo makes him a witness, not a suspect.
suspects.description: Devraj M is a person of interest in this case. Their motives and involvement remain under investigation.

items.label: Module ready. Tested on isolated clone. Confirm payment before I hand over the trigger.
items.detail: Recovered from a dark web escrow forum. Ronak S negotiated the exploit sale under the handle 'NullVector'. Linguistic analysis and PGP key confirm NullVector = Ronak S.

items.label: Abort. Shut it down now. This is going too far.
items.detail: Sent at 2:47 AM — 4 hours into the blackout, 2 hours before the buyer expected full escalation. Ronak S panicked and remotely triggered the shutdown sequence, cutting the attack short.

items.label: Recommend immediate patch of CVE-GRID-0091. Risk level: critical. This will be exploited.
items.detail: Devraj M warned about this exact vulnerability 2 years ago. His memo was classified and shelved. He is a witness to negligence, not a suspect.

items.label: Dr. Priya submitted a paper on GridCore vulnerabilities 6 months ago. She knows the internals better than anyone.
items.detail: Dr. Priya T published a peer-reviewed paper on GridCore SCADA security 6 months ago and was initially flagged as a suspect due to her deep technical knowledge. However, her paper used only publicly documented GridCore interfaces — not the proprietary memory layout used in the exploit. Her SDK licence was also never activated for the specific firmware version targeted. She was cleared after the SCADA access log confirmed she had not accessed the relevant repository files.
items.irrelevantReason: Dr. Priya T's knowledge came from public documentation. The exploit used proprietary memory layout only accessible via Ronak S's SDK licence.

items.label: zeroday_exploit_analysis.pdf
items.detail: Reverse-engineered exploit code targeting CVE-2024-XXXX — a buffer overflow in GridCore SCADA firmware version 4.2.1, present in substations across 3 states. The exploit achieves arbitrary code execution via a malformed UDP packet on port 47808 (BACnet). The code is written in C with inline assembly for the ROP chain. What distinguishes this from a generic exploit: the author demonstrated knowledge of GridCore's proprietary memory layout that is not documented in any public source. This layout knowledge could only come from access to GridCore's internal SDK — restricted to licensed researchers. Of the 3 researchers with SCADA codebase access at this facility, only Ronak S also held an active GridCore SDK licence. His licence was revoked 2 weeks after this exploit was first observed on dark web markets.

items.label: mauritius_account_trace.pdf
items.detail: $280,000 transferred in 3 tranches across 8 weeks to a Mauritius-registered shell account (AXIS-OFFSHORE-7741). Tranche details: $80,000 on Week 2 (2 days after the zero-day was confirmed viable in testing), $100,000 on Week 5 (after SCADA deployment code was delivered), $100,000 on Week 8 (after remote trigger capability was verified). Each transfer originated from a UAE-based intermediary account and passed through two additional jurisdictions before reaching Mauritius. The payments were structured below the $10,000 FATF reporting threshold per transaction. Cross-referencing the Mauritius account's beneficial ownership registry (obtained via MLAT) identifies the account controller as using Ronak S's passport number as the KYC document.

items.label: codebase_access_log.txt
items.detail: SCADA codebase access log from the research facility's version control system. The GridCore firmware repository contains the proprietary memory layout that was used in the exploit's ROP chain. Three researchers had read access: Ronak S, Dr. Priya T, and Ajay M. Access logs show Ronak S cloned the full repository at 11:47 PM on a Saturday — outside normal working hours. He accessed specifically the memory_map.h and firmware_structs.h header files, which contain the exact memory offsets used in the exploit. Dr. Priya T's last access was 3 weeks prior and limited to the configuration module. Ajay M had his access suspended 2 months ago due to a leave of absence. The Saturday night clone is the only anomalous access event in the 6-month log window.

hiddenItem.label: nullvector_pgp_identity.txt
hiddenItem.detail: The PGP public key used by forum handle 'NullVector' on the dark web exploit marketplace was extracted and submitted to key servers for cross-referencing. The key's UID field contains the email address ronak.sec.research@proton.me. A Proton account under this address was created 4 months ago. The key's creation timestamp predates the first listing by 11 days. More significantly, the key's subkey fingerprint was cross-referenced against a key Ronak S used to sign a paper submitted to a security conference 8 months ago — the master key matches. Ronak S appears to have generated a new UID for the dark web identity but reused his existing PGP master key, creating a cryptographic link between his real identity and the NullVector alias.

minigame.title: Analyse Dark Web Listing Email
minigame.emailSubject: Re: GridCore 0-day — Proof of Concept Available
minigame.emailBody:
Interested parties,

A working exploit for CVE-2024-XXXX targeting GridCore SCADA v4.2.1 is available. PoC confirmed on live substations. Price: $280,000 USD in 3 tranches.

Contact via PGP only. Key on keyserver.

NullVector
redFlags: Onion-to proxy domain — dark web communication
Claims live substation testing — implies criminal access
Payment structure matches financial trace (3 tranches)
PGP key matches researcher's known key fingerprint
minigame.hint: This is a criminal solicitation. The domain, payment terms, and PGP key are all evidence.
hints: The onion-to proxy exposes a dark web communication.
The 3-tranche payment structure matches the financial forensics.
This must be reported — it is direct evidence of the sale.

onSuccess.message: BREAKTHROUGH — nullvector_pgp_identity.txt confirmed. The dark web alias NullVector's PGP key shares a master key with Ronak S's known identity. The two identities are the same person.

items.label: Controlled Shutdown Analysis
items.detail: The blackout was terminated via a shutdown command sent over the same channel as the exploit — remotely, from the attacker's side. This was not a defensive response by the grid operators. The attacker chose to stop. This suggests remorse or fear — not state-level discipline.

items.label: Code Authorship Analysis
items.detail: Automated stylometry analysis of the exploit code against 12 published SCADA security papers returns a 91.3% match to Ronak S's writing style. Second-highest match is 41%.

items.label: Jump Server Trace
items.detail: Attack was routed through a custom VPN terminating at IP 45.61.185.22 — a jump server in Singapore. Server was rented using a credit card in Ronak S's name at a past conference hotel.

timeline.title: Vulnerability Identified — Ignored
timeline.description: Devraj M files memo about CVE-GRID-0091. Shelved.

timeline.title: Codebase Access Pattern
timeline.description: Ronak S accesses buffer overflow module 14 times

timeline.title: Dark Web Negotiation
timeline.description: NullVector (Ronak S) auctions exploit on dark web forum

timeline.title: Payment Received
timeline.description: $280,000 in 3 tranches to Mauritius shell account

timeline.title: Exploit Triggered
timeline.description: Zero-day executed on GridCore SCADA system

timeline.title: Blackout Begins
timeline.description: Industrial zone loses power — 6 facilities offline

timeline.title: Attacker Self-Terminates
timeline.description: Ronak S sends abort command — panic shutdown

perfect.title: Case Solved!
perfect.subtitle: Ronak S exposed as the zero-day seller who panicked mid-attack.
perfect.label: CASE CLOSED

partial.title: Insufficient Evidence
partial.subtitle: Correct suspect but the chain is incomplete.
partial.label: PARTIAL

wrongAccusation.title: Wrong Suspect
wrongAccusation.subtitle: The real culprit was Ronak S.
wrongAccusation.label: CASE FAILED
